content.text: What it is
Dashboard-as-code is the practice of storing Grafana (or equivalent) dashboard definitions as
version-controlled files

— JSON models, Jsonnet templates, or Terraform resources — and deploying them through an automated CI/CD pipeline rather than clicking through a web UI. The dashboard state in production is always a deterministic artifact of what is committed to the repository.

A GitOps workflow extends this further: git becomes the single source of truth for dashboard state. Every desired change is expressed as a pull request, every deployment is triggered by a merge, and the live dashboards in each environment are always reconcilable with the files on the corresponding branch.
Why it matters

Dashboards built through the Grafana UI suffer the same problems as manually provisioned infrastructure: they drift between environments, they are lost when a server is rebuilt, and the person who built them is the only one who knows what they mean. A single engineer editing a production dashboard at 3 AM to diagnose an incident can introduce a change that silently breaks the alert that woke them up.

For agentic AI observability specifically, dashboards are critical operational documentation. The golden signals panels, eval drift charts, and token cost visualizations must be as reliable as the agents they monitor. Dashboard-as-code eliminates the "works on my Grafana" class of problems and makes dashboard changes auditable and reviewable.
Benefits
Reproducibility — any environment can be rebuilt from source, including dashboards.
Auditability — every dashboard change has an author, timestamp, PR description, and review comment in git history.
Safe iteration — a bad dashboard change is a PR that gets rejected or a one-line revert, not a manual undo through the Grafana UI.
Environment consistency — dev, staging, and prod dashboards are promoted from the same source, preventing configuration drift.
Reuse — Jsonnet libraries and Terraform modules allow a single panel definition to be composed into dozens of dashboards without copy-paste.
Trade-offs
Initial friction is higher than clicking through the UI. Engineers need to learn Jsonnet or the Grafana JSON model before they can make dashboard changes.
The feedback loop is longer: a UI change is visible immediately, while a code change requires a PR, CI run, and deploy.
Grafana's JSON model is verbose and sometimes difficult to read as source code, especially for complex visualizations.
Escaping UI-managed dashboards requires exporting and committing existing dashboards, which is a one-time migration effort.
How it works
A developer creates or modifies a dashboard definition file (Jsonnet, JSON, or a Terraform resource block) on a feature branch. They open a pull request. Automated CI runs
jsonnet fmt
linting and schema validation. A reviewer examines the diff. On merge to the target branch, CD deploys the rendered JSON to Grafana via the Grafana API or Terraform apply.
Grafana prevents manual UI edits from persisting by provisioning dashboards with
editable: false
in the provisioning config, or by configuring Terraform to overwrite any UI drift on the next apply.
Architecture
The diagram shows the full GitOps workflow from feature branch to production Grafana, including environment promotion.

attrs.alt: Dashboard-as-Code GitOps workflow: Feature Branch to Pull Request to Merge to main to CI Pipeline to Deploy to Grafana to Production, with environment promotion row below

content.text: When to use it
Any team operating multiple Grafana environments (dev, staging, prod) that must stay consistent.
Organizations with compliance or audit requirements that demand a record of who changed what and when.
Teams already using infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi) — dashboards are a natural extension of the same workflow.
When dashboard definitions need to be reviewed by someone other than the person who created them — for example, an SRE reviewing an agent engineer's new quality signals panel.
How to implement
Choose a definition format first. For teams comfortable with Terraform, the
grafana/grafana

Terraform provider is the lowest-friction path. For teams who want richer composition and reuse, Grafonnet (a Jsonnet library for Grafana) enables function-level abstraction. Raw Grafana JSON works but is verbose and not recommended for hand-editing.
Repository structure for a dashboard-as-code repo:

dashboards/
  lib/
    panels.libsonnet     # reusable panel definitions
    datasources.libsonnet
  agentic-ai/
    golden-signals.jsonnet
    eval-drift.jsonnet
    token-cost.jsonnet
  platform/
    infrastructure.jsonnet
terraform/
  main.tf               # grafana_dashboard resources
  variables.tf
.github/workflows/
  deploy-dashboards.yml
Examples
A minimal Grafonnet dashboard definition for an eval drift panel:
The equivalent Terraform resource using the Grafana provider:
The GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline that renders Jsonnet and applies Terraform:

# .github/workflows/deploy-dashboards.yml
name: Deploy Dashboards

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['dashboards/**', 'terraform/**']

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Jsonnet
        run: sudo apt-get install -y jsonnet

      - name: Render Jsonnet to JSON
        run: |
          mkdir -p build
          for f in dashboards/agentic-ai/*.jsonnet; do
            name=$(basename "$f" .jsonnet)
            jsonnet -J dashboards/lib "$f" > "build/${name}.json"
          done

      - name: Validate JSON schema
        run: |
          for f in build/*.json; do
            python3 scripts/validate_dashboard_schema.py "$f"
          done

      - name: Terraform Init
        working-directory: terraform
        run: terraform init
        env:
          GRAFANA_URL: ${{ secrets.GRAFANA_URL }}
          GRAFANA_AUTH: ${{ secrets.GRAFANA_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN }}

      - name: Terraform Apply
        working-directory: terraform
        run: terraform apply -auto-approve
        env:
          GRAFANA_URL: ${{ secrets.GRAFANA_URL }}
          GRAFANA_AUTH: ${{ secrets.GRAFANA_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN }}
Real-world example

A platform team managing observability for five agentic AI products migrates their 30 Grafana dashboards to Grafonnet. Each product team submits dashboard changes through PRs. The platform team reviews for naming conventions and datasource consistency. After merge, a GitHub Actions workflow renders the Jsonnet, validates the resulting JSON against a Grafana schema, and runs
terraform apply
against the production Grafana instance.
When a product engineer accidentally removes the eval drift panel during an incident, the on-call engineer does not manually recreate it. They run
terraform apply
locally from the main branch and the panel is restored in under 30 seconds. The git log shows that the panel was correctly defined the entire time.
Common mistakes

Leaving UI editing enabled in Grafana. Engineers will make UI edits during incidents and never commit them. Configure provisioning with editable: false or rely on Terraform overwrite to prevent drift from persisting.
Committing raw Grafana JSON exports without any abstraction. Hundreds of lines of generated JSON are unreadable in code review and unmaintainable over time.
Using a single branch for all environments. Without environment branches (dev/staging/main), every change goes to production immediately, eliminating the ability to test in staging.
Skipping the schema validation step. A malformed dashboard JSON will either fail silently in Grafana or overwrite a working dashboard with a broken one.
Hardcoding datasource names instead of using Grafana template variables. Hardcoded names break when promoting from dev to staging, where datasource UIDs differ.
Best practices
Set stable UIDs for dashboards (the uid field in the JSON model). Without a stable UID, each Terraform apply creates a new dashboard instead of updating the existing one.
Use Grafonnet library functions for shared panel types (timeseries, stat, table) so the style is consistent across dashboards without copy-paste.
Require at least one reviewer from the platform team on dashboard PRs to catch query mistakes and maintain naming conventions.
Run jsonnet fmt in CI and fail the build on formatting violations. This prevents style debates in reviews and keeps diffs minimal.
Tag every dashboard with its owning team and service in the tags array so dashboards are searchable and attributable in the Grafana UI.
Performance and scalability
Terraform state locking and the Grafana API are the two bottlenecks at scale. When a monorepo contains hundreds of dashboards, a single Terraform apply can be slow and contentious. Mitigations:
Use targeted applies:
terraform apply -target grafana_dashboard.eval_drift
instead of applying the whole state.
Split dashboard state into per-team Terraform workspaces or modules so teams do not block each other on state locks.
Use the Grafana provisioning filesystem approach (mount a configmap) rather than the API for environments where the Grafana pod can be restarted cheaply — this avoids API rate limits entirely.
Tooling and ecosystem
Grafonnet — the canonical Jsonnet library for Grafana dashboards, maintained by the Grafana Labs team. Provides typed builder functions for every panel type.
Terraform grafana/grafana provider — deploys dashboards, folders, alerts, and datasources via Terraform apply. Version 3.x supports Grafana 10+ features.
grafana-dashboard-export CLI — exports existing UI-built dashboards to JSON for migration into the GitOps workflow.
GitHub Actions / GitLab CI — the standard automation layer for PR validation and deployment pipelines.
Grizzly — an alternative CLI tool from Grafana Labs that manages Grafana resources declaratively without Terraform, useful for simpler setups.
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Reusability
Review flow

Learning curve
UI editing (no code)
None
None
Manual copy
None
Raw JSON in git
Low
PR diff
Branch merge
Low
Grafonnet (Jsonnet)
High
PR diff
Branch merge

Terraform provider
Medium

Security and reliability
Store Grafana service account tokens as CI secrets, never in the repository. Use short-lived tokens with the minimum required permissions (dashboard editor role on the specific folder).
Protect the main branch with required reviews and status checks. A direct push to main bypasses the validation pipeline and could deploy a broken dashboard to production.
The Terraform state file contains Grafana resource IDs. Store it in a backend with encryption at rest and access logging.
Pin tool versions in CI (Jsonnet, Terraform, provider) to prevent unexpected behavior changes from upstream updates breaking the pipeline.
Mental model

Think of Grafana dashboards as the same category of artifact as a Kubernetes Deployment manifest or an nginx config file. You would not hand-edit those in production without a review and a record. Dashboards that monitor production AI agents are just as critical — an incorrect panel can mislead the on-call engineer who relies on it during an incident.

The GitOps model — git as the single source of truth, automated reconciliation from repo to live system — is already the standard for infrastructure. Dashboard-as-code applies the same principle one layer up the stack: the observability layer itself becomes as reproducible and auditable as the infrastructure it monitors.
Key takeaways
Dashboard-as-code stores Grafana definitions in git, making dashboards reproducible, auditable, and peer-reviewable.
GitOps makes git the single source of truth: merges trigger deploys, and live state is always reconcilable with the repository.
Grafonnet (Jsonnet) provides composition and reuse that raw JSON export cannot.
The Terraform grafana/grafana provider is the lowest-friction path for teams already using Terraform for infrastructure.
Environment promotion (dev → staging → main) mirrors the application deployment model and prevents dashboard changes from reaching production untested.
Disable or overwrite UI editing in managed environments so the repo remains the authoritative state rather than a suggestion.
